verses.commentary: When the Sabbath was over, Mary Magdalene, Mary mother of James, and Salome bought spices to anoint Jesus' body. Sabbath rest observed. Now they can complete burial rites. Love and devotion persist beyond death.

Very early on the first day of the week, just after sunrise, they were on their way to the tomb. First day = Sunday. New beginning. Creation week began on the first day; new creation begins on the first day. Dawn.
They asked each other: Who will roll the stone away from the entrance? Practical concern. They expect a sealed tomb. They lack strength for the task but come anyway.
But when they looked up, they saw the stone, which was very large, had been rolled away. Problem solved before they arrive. Divine intervention.

As they entered the tomb, they saw a young man dressed in a white robe sitting on the right side, and they were alarmed. Angel. White = heavenly. Right side = position of authority. Alarm: natural human response to the supernatural.

Don't be alarmed. You are looking for Jesus the Nazarene, who was crucified. He has risen! He is not here. See the place where they laid him. Perfect announcement. Crucified: his identity confirmed by his death. He has risen (ēgerthē, ἠγέρθη): passive voice, God raised him. The tomb is empty. The place is shown. Evidence offered.

But go, tell his disciples and Peter: He is going ahead of you into Galilee. There you will see him, just as he told you. Command to proclaim. And Peter: specific mention. Peter, who denied three times, is not excluded. Grace names the failure. Galilee: where it began, where it resumes. Just as he told you: 14:28. His word is reliable.

Trembling and bewildered, the women went out and fled from the tomb. They said nothing to anyone, because they were afraid. Mark's original ending (most scholars agree). Abrupt. Fear and silence. Trembling (tromos, τρόμος) and bewilderment (ekstasis, ἔκστασις, out of one's senses). The resurrection is not calm; it is shattering. They said nothing: disobedience from fear? Or temporary silence before eventual proclamation? Mark leaves readers hanging. We know they did speak eventually, or we wouldn't have the story. The abrupt ending forces the question: What will you do with the risen Jesus?

Verses 9–20 are the longer ending, absent from earliest manuscripts, likely added in the second century. Different vocabulary and style from Mark 1–16:8. Not original but ancient. When Jesus rose early on the first day, he appeared first to Mary Magdalene, out of whom he had driven seven demons. First witness: a woman previously demon-possessed. The kingdom inverts honor. The least become the greatest messengers.
She went and told those with him who were mourning and weeping. She obeys. Disciples grieve, still not expecting resurrection.
When they heard Jesus was alive and she had seen him, they did not believe. Unbelief persists. Even testimony from a known disciple is dismissed. Resurrection was inconceivable.

Afterward Jesus appeared in a different form to two while walking in the country. Emmaus road (Luke 24:13–35). Different form: unrecognizable initially. Resurrection body both continuous and transformed.
They returned and reported to the rest, but they did not believe them either. Second testimony rejected. Skepticism deep.

Later Jesus appeared to the Eleven as they were eating. He rebuked them for their lack of faith and stubborn refusal to believe those who had seen him after he had risen. Jesus confronts unbelief. Rebuke, not comfort. Faith requires evidence, but evidence does not produce automatic faith. Hard hearts resist.

He said to them: Go into all the world and preach the gospel to all creation. The Great Commission. Universal scope. All the world, all creation. The gospel is for everyone, everywhere. Barriers removed.
Whoever believes and is baptized will be saved, but whoever does not believe will be condemned. Faith is the requirement. Baptism is the sign. Belief saves; unbelief condemns. Binary outcome.

These signs will accompany those who believe: In my name they will drive out demons, speak in new tongues. Miraculous signs validate the gospel. Exorcism, languages. Acts of the Apostles demonstrates these.

They will pick up snakes with their hands; when they drink deadly poison, it will not hurt them; they will place hands on sick people, and they will get well. Protection from harm (Acts 28:3–6), healing. Not prescriptive (test God by drinking poison), but descriptive (God protects his messengers). Signs authenticate the message.

After the Lord Jesus had spoken to them, he was taken up into heaven and sat at the right hand of God. Ascension. Psalm 110:1 fulfilled. Exaltation. The suffering Servant becomes the reigning King. Session at God's right hand: co-regency, authority, intercession.

Then the disciples went out and preached everywhere, and the Lord worked with them and confirmed his word by the signs that accompanied it. Obedience. Universal proclamation. Divine partnership. The Lord worked with them: Jesus continues his ministry through his disciples. Signs confirm the word. The mission begins. Mark ends with the church on mission. The gospel must go to all nations (13:10). The reader is invited to join. The abrupt original ending at 16:8 asks: Will you believe and proclaim? The longer ending shows: They did, and so must we. Chapter sixteen: empty tomb, resurrection announcement, fear, unbelief, rebuke, commission, ascension, mission. He is risen. The King lives. The story continues. The gospel spreads. Go and tell.
